data.title: The FTSE MIB Index Closes 1.48% Higher
data.description:
In Milan, the FTSE MIB Index went up by 540 points or 1.48 percent on Thursday.

Top gainers were Buzzi Unicem (6.87%), Bper Banca (4.54%) and Banco BPM SPA (4.52%).

data.title: SUBURBAN PROPANE PARTNERS LP SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
Suburban Propane Partners LP, a leading distributor of propane, fuel oil, and related products and services, has released its Form 10-Q report for the first quarter of fiscal 2025. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial and operational performance, highlighting key metrics and challenges faced during the period.

Financial Highlights

Total Revenues: $373.3 million, reflecting a 2.0% increase compared to the prior year first quarter.

Gross Margin: $226.2 million, increased by $13.4 million or 6.3% compared to the prior year first quarter, driven by a $3.6 million unrealized gain from derivative instruments.

Operating Income: $59.1 million, up from $48.7 million in the prior year first quarter, indicating improved operational efficiency.

Net Income: $19.4 million, down from $24.5 million in the prior year first quarter, impacted by lower heat-related demand and agricultural demand.

Net Income Per Common Unit - basic: $0.30, compared to $0.38 in the prior year first quarter, reflecting the decrease in net income.

Business Highlights

Revenue Segments: The propane segment remains the largest contributor to revenue, with significant sales to residential, commercial, industrial, government, and agricultural customers. The fuel oil and refined fuels segment, as well as the natural gas and electricity segment, also contribute to the overall revenue, albeit to a lesser extent.

Geographical Performance: The company experienced varied demand across its service territories, with the Southeast region seeing increased demand following Hurricanes Helene and Milton. However, overall demand was impacted by unseasonably warm temperatures, particularly in November, which was one of the warmest on record.

Sales Units: Retail propane gallons sold in the first quarter of fiscal 2025 were 105.7 million gallons, a slight decrease of 0.8% compared to the prior year, primarily due to lower heat-related demand and reduced agricultural demand for crop drying.

Operational Challenges: The company faced inflationary pressures affecting the costs of goods and services, including labor, transportation, and capital expenditures. These factors, along with competition for resources, have impacted operational costs and consumer demand.

Future Outlook: Management anticipates continued challenges from inflation and supply chain pressures but remains focused on customer base growth and retention initiatives to drive future performance. The company is also monitoring regulatory changes in New York that could impact its natural gas and electricity business.

SEC Filing: SUBURBAN PROPANE PARTNERS LP [ SPH ] - 10-Q - Feb. 06, 2025

data.title: Air Products & Chemicals, Inc. SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
Air Products & Chemicals, Inc., a leading global industrial gases company, has released its Form 10-Q report for the third quarter of the fiscal year. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial performance and operational activities, reflecting the challenges and opportunities faced in the current market environment.

Financial Highlights

Sales: $2,931.5 million, a decrease of 2% from the prior year due to lower volumes and unfavorable currency impact, partially offset by favorable pricing.

Gross Margin: 31.2%, an increase of 20 basis points from the prior year as pricing and favorable business mix more than offset the impact of higher costs.

Operating Income: $643.6 million, a decrease of 3% due to higher costs and lower volumes, partially offset by positive pricing and favorable currency.

Net Income: $649.8 million, an increase of 5% as higher pricing and lower non-service pension costs were partially offset by higher costs.

Diluted EPS: $2.77, an increase of 1% from the prior year, reflecting higher pricing and a gain on de-designated cash flow hedges.

Business Highlights

Revenue Segments

  • Americas: $1,287.6 million
  • Asia: $817.1 million
  • Europe: $697.2 million
  • Middle East and India: $32.8 million
  • Corporate and other: $96.8 million

Sales were primarily driven by on-site and merchant supply modes, with a notable sale of equipment in the Corporate and other segment.

Geographical Performance

The Americas segment experienced a significant, non-recurring sale of helium to an existing merchant customer, which partially offset lower volumes in other regions. The Europe segment faced lower contributions from on-sites and merchant supply modes, while the Asia segment maintained stable performance.

Sales Units

The company experienced a 2% decrease in sales volumes, primarily due to the divestiture of the LNG business and lower contributions from on-sites and merchant supply modes in Europe. However, this was partially offset by a significant helium sale in the Americas.

New Production Launches

The NEOM Green Hydrogen Project in Saudi Arabia is a key focus, with ongoing construction and financing activities. The project is expected to significantly contribute to future operational capacity and revenue streams.

Future Outlook

Management anticipates realizing significant benefits from the U.S. Inflation Reduction Act of 2022, particularly from Section 48 energy investment tax credits, once qualifying projects come onstream. The company is also evaluating the impact of new accounting guidance and climate-related disclosure requirements on future operations.

SEC Filing: Air Products & Chemicals, Inc. [ APD ] - 10-Q - Feb. 06, 2025

data.title: Koncar, Podravka weigh on Croatian stock indices
data.description:
ZAGREB (Croatia), February 6 (SeeNews) - The two main share indices of the Zagreb Stock Exchange marginally declined as gains in hotel owner and manager Valamar [ZSE:RIVP] failed to offset losses in electrical concern Koncar [ZSE:KOEI] and food and drugs manufacturer Podravka [ZSE:PODR], bourse data showed on Thursday.

The bourse’s 19-share equity benchmark, the CROBEX, dipped 0.05% to 3,461.51 points.

The narrower blue-chip CROBEX10 index went down 0.16% to 2,183.23 points.

Shares of index heavyweight Koncar shed 0.92% to 540 euro ($560) in a turnover of 399,000 euro. They soared 11.57% in the last one month and 157% int he last one year.

Valamar ranked second by daily turnover with 206,000 euro as its shares appreciated 1.30% to 6.24 euro.

Podravka dropped 1.72% to 142.50 euro in a turnover of 111,000 euro.

The bourse's regular turnover fell to 1.7 million euro from 2.1 million euro a day earlier.

($ = 0.964 euro)

data.title: Weekly Jobless Claims Rise; January Job Cuts Increase Sequentially
data.description:
Weekly applications for unemployment insurance in the US rose more than expected, according to government data, while a separate report showed that job cuts in the country increased sequentially in January.

The seasonally adjusted number of initial claims increased by 11,000 to 219,000 in the week ended Feb. 1, the Department of Labor reported Thursday. The consensus was for a 213,000 level in a survey of analysts compiled by Bloomberg. The previous week's reading was revised up by 1,000 to 208,000.

The four-week moving average came in at 216,750, advancing by 4,000 from the prior average that was revised up by 250. Weekly unadjusted claims climbed by 11,370 to 239,690.

"Despite the modest increase in the week ended (Feb. 1), initial claims for unemployment insurance benefits remain low and not appreciably higher than this time last year," Oxford Economics Chief US Economist Ryan Sweet said in remarks emailed to MT Newswires. "Initial claims can be choppy but the four-week moving average remained comfortably below our estimate of its break-even level, or that consistent with no monthly job growth."

Seasonally adjusted continuing claims totaled 1.89 million for the week ended Jan. 25, ahead of the Bloomberg consensus of about 1.87 million. Continuing claims jumped by 36,000 from the previous week's level, which was revised down by 8,000. The four-week moving average came in at 1.87 million, increasing by 2,250 from the prior week's downwardly revised average, according to the DOL.

"Continuing claims for unemployment insurance — a proxy for hiring — resumed its increase in the week ended (Jan. 25)," according to Sweet. "This is consistent with decline in the job finding rate and the rise in duration of unemployment."

On Friday, the Bureau of Labor Statistics is expected to report that the US economy added 170,000 nonfarm jobs in January, which would mark a slowdown from the 256,000 gain posted for the month prior, according to a Bloomberg poll.

US-based employers cut 49,795 jobs last month, up 28% from December, but down 40% annually, according to a separate report released Thursday by Challenger Gray & Christmas. This was the lowest January layoffs total since 2022. Among industries, the technology sector lost the most number of jobs.

"January was relatively quiet in terms of job cut announcements," Andrew Challenger, senior vice president of Challenger Gray & Christmas, said. "However, we've already seen major announcements in the early days of February, so it seems this quiet is unlikely to last."

data.title: Qualcomm and Arm Can Gain From DeepSeek. They Need 1 Thing to Change. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Adam Clark

Qualcomm and Arm Holdings executives are the latest to say the prospect of low-cost artificial intelligence is a good thing. The two chip companies have a decent argument, but they need sales of AI smartphones to take off.

Practically every chip and technology company to report results this earnings season has said that DeepSeek's demonstration of low-cost AI development is actually a good thing for the sector, even though a panic about the Chinese startup's advances wiped away nearly $1 trillion of value from the U.S. stock market.

Qualcomm, a specialist in chips for mobile phones, and the semiconductor-design company Arm have a better argument than most. Both companies should benefit from more computing moving from the cloud onto devices, a way of operating known as "edge AI." More efficient AI models bring that future closer.

Qualcomm CEO Cristiano Amon told analysts on an earnings call Wednesday that DeepSeek models were running on smartphones and personal computers powered by the company's chips within a few days of its release. "DeepSeek-R1 and other similar models recently demonstrated that AI models are developing faster, becoming smaller, more capable and efficient, and now able to run directly on device," Amon said.

That is a shift that can't come quickly enough for Qualcomm, whose stock performance has trailed far behind peers such as Nvidia that provide chips to power AI data centers. The hoped-for boost to smartphone sales that edge AI could bring has been slow to arrive, and handset-related revenue makes up the majority of Qualcomm's business.

In Qualcomm's favor, its handset revenue rose 13% in the first quarter. That is way above the 2.4% overall growth in global phone shipments in the last quarter of 2024, as estimated by International Data Corporation.

"Like Apple, Qualcomm is seeing an uptick in its premium tier with those handset makers that introduced AI-related features. However, the impact of this positive trend could be a little more muted in 2H25 as we prepare for the loss of Apple content in the September quarter and beyond," wrote Melius Research analyst Ben Reitzes in a research note.

Beginning this fall, Apple will begin to substitute 5G chips of its own making for those made by Qualcomm.

Reitzes kept a Hold rating on Qualcomm stock but raised his target price to $190 from $180. Qualcomm shares were down 4.9% at $167.28 in morning trading.

Meanwhile, Arm CEO Rene Haas told analysts that DeepSeek's demonstration of cheaper AI was "terrific" for the company in its own earnings call.

"As wonderful a product as [Nvidia's] Grace Blackwell [chip] is, you'd never be able to put it in a cellphone, you'd never be able to put it into earbuds. You can't even put it into a car. But Arm is in all those places," Haas said.

Arm isn't quite as dependent on smartphones as Qualcomm. Although it does plenty of business licensing its designs for handsets, it also has a growing position in high-end cloud-server processors and in AI-enabled PCs. Still, more AI smartphone sales would promote the use of Arm's latest advanced chip technology, called Armv9, which generates double the royalty rates of the previous version.

"We...view the emergence of AI Agents and efficient AI models (such as DeepSeek) as a significant positive for ARM given its strong presence at the edge," wrote Raymond James analyst Srini Pajjuri in a research note.

Pajjuri raised his target price on Arm to $175 from $160 and kept an Outperform rating on the stock.

Write to Adam Clark at adam.clark@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Urals diffs stable, freight rates in Russia's East ease
data.description:
Urals crude differentials were steady on Thursday, while freight rates for Russia's oil loading from Far East Kozmino port eased amid higher availability, traders said.

Freight rates for ESPO Blend crude shipped from the Russian port of Kozmino to China have fallen 50% in early February from mid-January levels as a shortage of tankers following U.S. sanctions on the fleet shipping Russian oil eased, four traders said.

Freight rates for Russian oil loading from its western ports remained relatively stable as availability of the cargoes was good, traders said.

Traders expected Azeri BTC oil loading plan for March to be released on Friday.

PLATTS WINDOW

  • No bids or offers were shown for Urals, Azeri BTC and CPC Blend in the Platts window on Thursday.

NEWS

  • China's retaliatory tariffs on the United States may cause U.S. oil exports to decline in 2025 for the first time since the COVID-19 pandemic, after growth plateaued last year.

data.title: Global Commodities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Commodities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1208 ET - Base metal prices are mixed, with LME three-month copper up 0.2% at $9,283.50 a metric ton and LME three-month aluminum flat at $2,624.50 a ton. Base metals are holding ground as investors await further clarity around trade tensions, with copper up 2.25% on-week and aluminum up just 0.1%. Metals were pressured lower on Monday after U.S. President Trump imposed tariffs on Canada, Mexico and China, before bouncing back later in the session after the Mexican and Canadian tariffs were put on hold for one month. The Chinese tariffs remain in place, and China has vowed to retaliate with tariffs of its own with an implementation date of Feb. 10. This economic uncertainty has damped investor appetite for industrial metals, as tariffs will be negative for global economic growth. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

1052 ET - U.S. natural gas inventories declined by 174 billion cubic feet last week, in line with the five-year average for the period, the EIA reports. At 2,397 Bcf, gas in underground storage at the end of January was 111 Bcf below the five-year average and 208 Bcf below the year-ago level. Analysts in a Wall Street Journal survey had forecast a 170 Bcf drawdown for last week. Nymex natural gas is off 1.2% at $3.319/mmBtu, supported on the downside by some colder weather foreseen for mid-February. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1019 ET - Live cattle futures are down 1.8% in early trading, bringing the most-active CME contract down below the $2/lb mark. Fund traders have been net long on cattle due to the expectations of tight supply versus strong demand, but these traders appear to be cutting their exposure to long positions as uncertainty due to tariff threats and other initiatives out of the Trump administration. Longer-term, cattle prices are expected to keep rising amid strong demand for U.S. beef overseas and light slaughter rates domestically. Lean hog futures are down 0.4% in early trading. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1009 ET - Hershey said its volume of chocolate sales decreased in its most recent quarter, which CEO Michele Buck attributed in part to convenience stores facing pressure. Consumers also continue to be focused on health and wellness, she said, with the chocolatier seeing growth in zero-sugar and protein products. Successful innovation in the sweets category has also contributed, Buck said, highlighting Shaq-a-licious sour gummies and freeze dried Jolly Ranchers as two recent examples, and will come to Reese's Peanut Butter Cups later this year. (roshan.fernandez@wsj.com)

0955 ET - Aurubis' first quarter is likely to have been the strongest period of fiscal 2025, Warburg analyst Stefan Augustin says in a research note. The German supplier of non-ferrous metal and recycler of copper stated that the first quarter was positively influenced by a significantly higher metal result, more than compensating for a year-over-year drop in treatment and refining charges. The anticipated lower treatment and refining charges will be felt from next quarter onward, Augustin says. Copper concentrate treatment and refining charges--also known as TC/RCs--are paid by mining companies to smelters. Shares trade 5.1% higher at 78.50 euros. (nina.kienle@wsj.com)

0955 ET - Natural resources companies should weather the tariff storm if imposed, Morningstar DBRS suggests. Although U.S. tariffs on imports from Canada and Mexico have been delayed, the threat remains. Morningstar says there would be short-term disruptions in trade flows until supply chains are rebalanced if the levies are imposed. Yet it doesn't expect mining companies to scale back production and would instead deliver on operational guidance provided for 2025. The robust commodity prices that have persisted over the past few years have allowed mining companies to significantly reduce debt levels and consequently they have strong credit metrics, Morningstar says. (robb.stewart@wsj.com)

0950 ET - Oil futures are recouping some of the previous day's losses, while the market continues to ponder the impact of trade tariffs, rising U.S. crude stocks, and prospects of a step-up in OPEC+ output in April. "Most traders feel the U.S. tariff threats to China are for real and could damage their economy further, and with OPEC set to raise production soon, WTI prices may have heavier headwinds in the future," BOK Financial's Dennis Kissler says in a note. "Look for a choppy type trade until more is known about tariffs." WTI is up 0.7% at $71.53 a barrel, and Brent is 0.6% higher at $75.02.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0949 ET - Cocoa alternatives are in growing demand given high prices for the real thing, Hershey CEO Michele Buck said. Some companies feeling the pressure have switched to cocoa butter alternatives, she said, reducing demand in the global cocoa market. "We do that where possible, but we're pretty precious about the brands and what they stand for with consumers, " Buck said. (roshan.fernandez@wsj.com)

0948 ET - U.S. natural gas futures are lower ahead of the EIA's weekly storage report, but holding ground with some colder weather returning to mid-February forecasts. Natural gas storage likely fell by 170 Bcf to 2,401 Bcf in the last week of January, according to a Wall Street Journal survey, leaving stocks at a 107 Bcf deficit to the five-year average. The Nymex front month is down 1.3% at $3.317/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0947 ET - Steelmaker ArcelorMittal says it intends to build a plant in Alabama to produce the paper-thin steel used in the motors of electric vehicles, air conditioners and other products. Construction of the $1.2-billion plant at ArcelorMittal's steel mill near Mobile, Ala., will start later this year. Production is projected to begin in 2027. The plant will be able to produce about 165,000 tons a year. Production of electric vehicles is expected to drive higher demand for electrical steel, which accounts for about 1% of the worldwide steel production annually with most of it now in Asia. The only U.S. producers of electrical steel are Cleveland-Cliffs and U.S. Steel, which recently began production in Arkansas. (robert.tita@wsj.com )

0907 ET - Arabica coffee futures rise to a record high on supply worries. ICE coffee futures rose 1.7% to around $4.05 a pound in European afternoon trading. They have climbed as high as $4.11 earlier in the session. The recent price rises reflect persistent supply concerns, especially in Brazil, ING analysts say in a note. There are suggestions that market conditions will remain tight as exchange inventories continue to fall, while the next harvest is set to suffer from bad weather, ING says. Adverse weather prior to October deteriorated harvest prospects for the crop, and market participants have reported bleak forecasts. Coffee futures have notched successive highs in nearly every session since mid-January. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0900 ET - Western brands lost sales in their stores doing business in the Middle East after the outbreak of the Israel-Hamas war in October 2023, but some are seeing improvements. Fast food owner Yum Brands says transactions in its KFC restaurants in the Middle East rebounded in 4Q compared to before the conflict, with same-store sales in the region up 21%. Yum is up 3% premarket on a slight adjusted 4Q EPS beat. (heather.haddon@wsj.com; @heatherhaddon)

data.title: HUNTINGTON INGALLS INDUSTRIES, INC. SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Huntington Ingalls Industries, Inc. (HII), a global defense partner specializing in building naval ships and technologies, has released its annual 10-K report. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial performance, business operations, strategic initiatives, and the challenges it faces in the current market environment.

Financial Highlights

Sales and service revenues: $11,535 million, reflecting a 1% increase from the previous year due to higher volumes at Mission Technologies and Ingalls, partially offset by lower volumes at Newport News.

Cost of sales and service revenues: $10,085 million, representing a 3% increase from the previous year, driven by higher volumes at Ingalls in surface combatants and higher volumes in the Columbia class program at Newport News.

Operating income: $535 million, a decrease of 31% from the previous year, primarily due to lower performance on the Virginia class submarine program and aircraft carriers.

Net earnings: $550 million, a decrease of 19% from the previous year, impacted by lower operating income and changes in tax rates.

Basic earnings per share: $13.96, reflecting a decrease from $17.07 in the previous year, due to lower net earnings.

Diluted earnings per share: $13.96, consistent with basic earnings per share, reflecting the same decrease from the previous year.

Business Highlights

Business Overview: Huntington Ingalls Industries, Inc. (HII) operates through three segments: Ingalls Shipbuilding, Newport News Shipbuilding, and Mission Technologies. The company specializes in building naval ships and integrated technology solutions.

Ingalls Shipbuilding: Ingalls is the sole builder of amphibious assault ships and large multi-mission national security cutters for the U.S. Coast Guard. It is also one of two builders of surface combatants for the U.S. Navy.

Newport News Shipbuilding: Newport News is the exclusive designer and builder of nuclear-powered aircraft carriers and one of two companies capable of designing and building nuclear-powered submarines for the U.S. Navy.

Mission Technologies: This segment focuses on integrated technology solutions, including command, control, communications, cyber, intelligence, surveillance, and reconnaissance systems, as well as uncrewed systems and fleet sustainment.

Revenue Segments: Ingalls reported increased volumes in surface combatants, while Newport News experienced lower volumes in aircraft carriers and naval nuclear support services. Mission Technologies saw higher volumes in cyber and electronic warfare and C5ISR contracts.

Sales Units: Ingalls delivered USS Jack H. Lucas (DDG 125) in 2023 and is constructing several other ships, including Ted Stevens (DDG 128) and Jeremiah Denton (DDG 129). Newport News delivered USS George Washington (CVN 73) after its refueling and complex overhaul.

New Product Launches: Ingalls is constructing new amphibious assault ships, including Bougainville (LHA 8) and Fallujah (LHA 9), and has been awarded contracts for future ships like Helmand Province (LHA 10).

New Production Launches: Newport News is involved in the construction of Columbia class (SSBN 826) submarines, with contracts awarded for additional boats in the class.

Future Outlook: The company anticipates continued demand for its shipbuilding and technology solutions, driven by global geopolitical tensions and U.S. defense priorities. Challenges include labor availability, supply chain disruptions, and inflationary pressures.

Strategic Initiatives

Strategic Initiatives: The company has focused on strategic initiatives to enhance its operational efficiency and market position. This includes the construction of new shipbuilding programs such as the Columbia class submarines and the San Antonio class amphibious transport dock ships. Additionally, the company has been awarded significant contracts, including the maintenance and overhaul of USS Boise and the construction of USS Richard J. Danzig, which align with its strategic focus on expanding its defense offerings.

Capital Management: In 2024, the company issued $1 billion in senior notes to strengthen its capital structure, with proceeds intended for general corporate purposes, including debt repayment and working capital. The company also increased its stock repurchase program to $3.8 billion and extended it to 2028, repurchasing 607,841 shares at a cost of $163 million. Furthermore, the company increased its quarterly dividend to $1.35 per share, reflecting a commitment to returning value to shareholders.

Future Outlook: Looking ahead, the company plans to continue its focus on strategic capital investments, with expected capital expenditures for maintenance and sustainment at approximately 1.5% of annual revenues. The company also anticipates discretionary capital expenditures to be around 2.0% to 2.5% of annual revenues. Additionally, the company expects to maintain its current debt obligations through internally generated funds and existing borrowing facilities, while continuing to explore opportunities for strategic growth and capital deployment.

Challenges and Risks

Challenges and Risks: Huntington Ingalls Industries, Inc. (HII) is heavily reliant on contracts with the U.S. Government, primarily the Department of Defense (DoD). This dependency poses significant risks due to potential changes in government priorities, budget constraints, and procurement processes. The company's operations are also subject to intense competition within the shipbuilding industry, which could impact market share and profitability.

Industry and Economic Risks: HII faces several industry and economic risks, including dependence on U.S. Government contracts, significant delays or reductions in appropriations for programs, changes in DoD business practices and procurement processes, intense competition and bid protests, high levels of indebtedness, and classified contracts limiting investor insight into certain business areas.

Business and Operational Risks: These include cost growth on flexibly priced contracts, challenges in recruiting and retaining qualified personnel, dependence on subcontractor performance and raw material availability, the need to increase shipbuilding capacity, risks associated with innovative designs and new technologies, cybersecurity threats, utilization of artificial intelligence, natural and environmental disasters, and health epidemics and pandemics.

Legal and Regulatory Risks: HII is heavily regulated as a U.S. Government contractor, with potential adverse effects from changes in regulations or negative audit findings. The company is also exposed to investigations, claims, and litigation, environmental costs and liabilities, risks associated with improper conduct by employees or partners, changes in tax laws, and challenges in protecting intellectual property rights.

General Risks: These include inadequate insurance coverage for significant risks, market volatility and adverse capital market conditions, and risks associated with acquisitions, joint ventures, and investments.

Management Strategies: Management acknowledges the challenges posed by the competitive environment and the need to adapt to changes in government procurement practices. Strategies include enhancing operational efficiency, investing in workforce development, and leveraging technology to maintain a competitive edge.

Market Risks: The company is exposed to market risks such as fluctuations in interest rates and inflation, which could impact contract costs and financial results. Management is focused on mitigating these risks through strategic financial planning and cost management initiatives.

SEC Filing: HUNTINGTON INGALLS INDUSTRIES, INC. [ HII ] - 10-K - Feb. 06, 2025

data.title: How Shifting Consumer Demand Patterns are Contributing to High Egg Prices
data.description:
DENVER, Feb. 06, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — U.S. consumers are facing a prolonged period of higher egg prices that will likely extend through the Easter holiday and well into 2025. Rising egg prices and increased volatility in the market are largely attributable to supply challenges brought on by Highly Pathogenic Avian Influenza. Since the current outbreak began impacting U.S. poultry farms in 2022, nearly 100 million table egg laying hens have been affected. 

However, HPAI is not the only factor contributing to the supply and demand imbalance driving egg prices higher. Consumer demand for eggs has skyrocketed in recent years, with per capita consumption growing 20% from 2016-2019. Demand has also shifted away from conventional eggs as more consumers are choosing cage-free and other types of specialty eggs – further complicating the supply challenges. As well, nine states have enacted laws that require eggs sold in their states to be from cage-free hens.

According to a new report from CoBank’s Knowledge Exchange, the increase in overall demand for eggs, combined with the growing preference for specialty eggs, is exacerbating the impact of tight supplies precipitated by HPAI. The confluence of all three factors is prolonging the timeline for bringing egg supply and demand into closer alignment. Until then, retail egg prices will remain elevated. 

“Egg demand was relatively stable in the early 2000s and seasonality played a much bigger role in peak demand periods than it does today,” said Brian Earnest, lead animal protein economist with CoBank. “While seasonality remains an influencing factor, egg use has grown dramatically over the last 20 years. “Eggs have become a staple item for innovation in quick-service restaurant entrees, and marketing trends like the emergence of all-day breakfast have significantly boosted egg demand.”  

Rising demand for cage-free eggs has also outpaced supply in recent years. Currently, more than 120 million or roughly 40% of the table egg layers in U.S. commercial flocks are housed in cage-free production systems. That compares with just 30 million layers housed in cage-free systems in 2015. While the growth in supply of cage-free eggs has been substantial, more will be needed to adequately meet demand projections. 

Total egg laying hen inventories, including conventionally raised hens, have not been substantially depleted from where they were at the beginning of the HPAI outbreak. Commercial operators who have been impacted have moved swiftly to repopulate hens. Through January 2025, the U.S. egg industry has 8% fewer egg-laying hens than it did two years ago. But HPAI has evolved to become a persistent, year-round threat to production. 

“The last widespread outbreak of HPAI in 2015 was largely seasonal with most cases occurring during the winter and spring migration periods for wild birds,” said Earnest. “That seasonality appears to be gone. During the current outbreak, HPAI has been detected in birds or other species nearly every month since the outbreak began in February 2022.”

Read the report, Surging Egg Prices are Being Driven by More than Bird Flu.

About CoBank

CoBank is a cooperative bank serving vital industries across rural America. The bank provides loans, leases, export financing and other financial services to agribusinesses and rural power, water and communications providers in all 50 states. The bank also provides wholesale loans and other financial services to affiliated Farm Credit associations serving more than 77,000 farmers, ranchers and other rural borrowers in 23 states around the country.

CoBank is a member of the Farm Credit System, a nationwide network of banks and retail lending associations chartered to support the borrowing needs of U.S. agriculture, rural infrastructure and rural communities. Headquartered outside Denver, Colorado, CoBank serves customers from regional banking centers across the U.S. and also maintains an international representative office in Singapore.

Corporate Communications

data.title: US 30-Year Mortgage Rate Eases for 3rd Consecutive Week
data.description:
The average rate on a 30-year fixed mortgage backed by Freddie Mac eased to 6.89% as of February 6th, declining for the third consecutive week from the highest level since early May 2024 of 7.04%.

The decrease aligned with declining long-dated Treasury yields amid soft economic data, easing trade concerns, and market expectations of further interest rate cuts by the Federal Reserve. “The 30-year fixed-rate mortgage decreased this week, now averaging 6.89%.

Mortgage rates have been stable over the last month and incoming data suggest the economy remains on firm footing.

Even though rates are higher compared to last year, the last two weeks of purchase applications are modestly above what was seen a year ago, indicating some latent demand in the market,” said Sam Khater, Freddie Mac’s Chief Economist.

data.title: Market Chatter: Shell, TotalEnergies Sign LNG Supply Deal With Egypt
data.description:
Shell and TotalEnergies signed liquefied natural gas deals with Egypt worth about $3 billion to supply 60 cargoes to satisfy demand for 2025, Reuters reported Thursday, citing three trading sources.

The country, which became a net natural gas importer last year, abandoned plans to supply Europe after a sharp drop in domestic production, the report said.

Shell declined to comment, and TotalEnergies and Egypt's petroleum ministry didn't respond immediately to requests for comment, Reuters said.

The companies and the ministry didn't respond immediately respond to requests for comment from MT Newswires.

(Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.)

data.title: European Stocks Close at Fresh Peaks
data.description:
European stocks closed sharply higher on Thursday, benefiting from the continuation of a positive earnings season and pullback in benchmark borrowing costs.

The Eurozone’s STOXX 40 jumped 1.7% to close at a near 25-year high of 5,362, and the pan-European STOXX 600 advanced 1.3% to close at a record high of 545.

Heavyweight banks led the gains despite mixed results for Dutch giant ING, with BBVA, Santander, and UniCredit soaring between 6% and 3.5%.

In the meantime, industrials performed well, with Saint Gobain adding 6.2% while Siemens gained 3.5%.

Outside the Euro Area, British stocks received a boost from a dovish vote tally in the BoE’s expected 25bps rate cut, in addition to a rebound for base metal miners and more strong results for pharmaceutical giants.

AstraZeneca took the spotlight with a 6.3% surge following its strong results, consistent with moves from rivals Novo Nordisk and GSK this week.

data.title: Nymex Overview: WTI Contract Posts Modest Gains After Setting Fresh 2025 Low — OPIS
data.description:
The NYMEX March West Texas Intermediate, which dipped to a 2025 low $70.66/bbl in Thursday morning trading, recovered to post a modest gain ahead of midday.

The shaky start came as President Trump reiterated his promise to reduce U.S. energy costs, saying that if oil prices decline "everything will follow."

But the Treasury department later in the morning said it was taking steps to target shadow networks responsible for moving Iranian oil to China. The agency said revenue from the exports have provided Iran with billions of dollars that it is using to destabilize the Mideast.

The March WTI contract was up by 24cts to $71.27/bbl in late morning trading and the April Brent crude contract added 20cts to $74.81/bbl after bouncing off a morning low of $74.24/bbl.

Citigroup on Wednesday said it believes chances that Brent prices will range from $60 to $65/bbl in the second half of 2025 have improved, adding that the Trump administration will do all that it can to reduce prices.

Trading in refined product futures was muted. The NYMEX March RBOB contract, which represents the last winter-grade contract for gasoline, was up by 2.34cts to $2.0741/gal.

U.S. spot gasoline prices were mixed, with California markets posting the strongest increases due to a rash of planned and unplanned maintenance work.

Cash prices for Los Angeles CARBOB were 3.25cts/gal higher and cash gasoline values in Northern California were up by more than 11cts/gal. East of the Rockies markets posted gains of 2-3.25cts/gal.

Gasoline could soon get a boost from the calendar. Several recent years have seen February motor fuel demand rise by 400,000 to 500,000 b/d and consumption could get a boost by forecasts of warmer weather across much of the U.S.

Distillate futures were fractionally higher near midday. The NYMEX March ULSD contract was 0.07ct higher at $2.3849/gal, despite more strength in European natural gas values. If that continues, the market could see more large natural gas users in Europe switching to diesel.

This content was created by Oil Price Information Service, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. OPIS is run independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

  • Reporting by Tom Kloza, tkloza@opisnet.com; Editing by Jeff Barber, jbarber@opisnet.com

data.title: Disney Likely Confident of Hitting Full-Year Guidance After Solid Fiscal Q1 Results, BofA Says
data.description:
Walt Disney's strong fiscal Q1 results will likely give the company's management confidence to reach their full-year targets, BofA Securities said in a note emailed Thursday.

The company kept its 2025 outlook of high-single-digit adjusted earnings per share growth, but BofA said the strong Q1 earnings along with easier comps in H2, launch of a new cruise ship and solid booking trends underpin Disney's 6% to 8% operating income growth target for its experiences segment.

Disney's fiscal Q1 operating income exceeded expectations, while revenue was "modestly below," the investment firm said, adding that adjusted EPS of $1.76 was well above BofA's estimate of $1.40.

Following the results, BofA raised the company's EPS forecast to $5.45 from $5.36 for 2025, $6.13 from $6.03 for 2026, and $6.85 from $6.71 for 2027.

BofA has a buy rating and $140 price objective on Disney.

data.title: The CAC 40 Index Closes 1.47% Higher
data.description:
In Paris, the CAC 40 Index gained 116 points or 1.47 percent on Thursday.

Gains were led by Societe Generale (13.49%), ArcelorMittal (13.01%) and Saint-Gobain (6.24%).

data.title: CMS Energy posts lower quarterly profit on higher operating costs
data.description:
U.S. electric and gas utility CMS Energy reported a drop in its fourth-quarter profit on Thursday due to higher operating expenses, and warned of supply chain issues arising from potential tariffs.

U.S. natural gas futures rose 44.5% in 2024, resulting in higher operating costs for utilities like CMS Energy.

The utility also said it faces a "bumped up" supply chain situation and is looking to "migrate" to domestic vendors to cope with the potential tariffs, which were initiated and later paused by U.S. President Donald Trump on Canada and Mexico.

The company in 2024 sourced about 6% of its natural gas supply from Canada.

CMS's interest charges in the quarter rose 4.5% to $180 million from the previous year, as a higher-for-longer interest rate environment has pushed borrowing costs for utilities that typically incur major capital expenditures.

Mild temperatures in service areas during the reporting period also hurt the demand for natural gas and electricity, the company said in a conference call.

Total operating expenses for the fourth quarter rose to $1.56 billion from $1.54 billion in the year-ago period, the company said.

Its revenue rose 2% from a year earlier to $1.99 billion in the quarter ended December 31, but missed analysts' average estimate of $2.22 billion, according to LSEG data.

CMS Energy raised its 2025 forecast for adjusted earnings to between $3.54 and $3.60 per share, versus $3.52 to $3.58 previously. Analysts had estimated them at $3.59 per share.

The utility firm also increased its annual dividend by 11 cents to $2.17 per share.

The Jackson, Michigan-based firm said net income available to common stockholders fell to $262 million or 87 cents per share in the fourth quarter, from $306 million or $1.05 per share in the same period last year.

data.title: BMO Looks at Provincial Impact in Canada From U.S. Trade Tariffs
data.description:
For Canada overall, the United States trade tariffs and retaliation that didn't go through Monday would have seriously impacted growth, said Bank of Montreal (BMO).

A couple of quarters of contraction would've been well within the realm of possibility, noted the bank. With little confidence given the lack of historical precedent, BMO estimates that the tariffs would reduce real gross domestic product growth by about two percentage points to roughly zero in 2025.

At the provincial level, direct exposure to U.S. trade varies across Canada, and so will the impact, stated the bank. British Columbia (B.C.) carries a relatively low share of goods exports in its economy, and roughly half of those are destined for markets outside the U.S., but some industries — for example forestry — will come under duress.

Alberta and Saskatchewan, because of hefty energy exports, carry the largest exposure — exports to the U.S. are 25%-to-36% of GDP — but, the lighter 10% tariff on such products, and the assumption that a portion will be passed to the U.S. consumer, leaves the impact to run smaller in these provinces — at least in the short run, pointed out BMO.

Non-energy U.S. export exposure is highest throughout Central Canada. In Ontario, for example, U.S. goods export top 17% of GDP with a wide range of industries exposed — for example autos, machinery, metals and consumer goods.

Quebec's industrial metals and manufactured goods will be vulnerable, as will Manitoba's diverse manufacturing base, added the bank. Atlantic Canada is susceptible because of a few highly-concentrated industries, even if the broad economic impact appears smaller — such as the Newfoundland & Labrador fishery, and Prince Edward Island packaged food goods.

All told, BMO expects the economic impact to hit hardest in Ontario and the rest of Central Canada, to significantly impact some concentrated industries in Atlantic Canada and B.C., while dealing a lesser immediate blow in oil-producing provinces
